title: The Greatest Commandment
subtitle: The lawyer's testing question, and the Lord's answer: Love

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of the testing crowd fall away.
One question remains, and only one: how do you love.
Quiet your heart now before the Lord who answers it.
gospel: The Pharisees, hearing that he had silenced the Sadducees,
gathered together against him in one company.
And one of them, a scribe and doctor of the Law,
came forward, tempting him, and asked:
Master, which is the great commandment in the Law?
Thou shalt love the Lord thy God
with thy whole heart, and with thy whole soul,
and with thy whole mind, and with thy whole strength.
This is the greatest and the first commandment.
And the second is like to this:
Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself.
On these two commandments dependeth the whole Law.
behold: They could not overcome him by reason, so they came to conquer him by their multitude.
See them gather in one company, a great crowd, to terrify him the more.
The Pharisees withdraw and the Sadducees come; the Sadducees withdraw and the Pharisees return,
wearing him down by their constant assault, contrary to one another in opinion,
yet of one mind in the persecution of Christ.
And one steps forward, a scribe, a doctor of the Law.
He calls him Master, though he does not wish to be his disciple.
He asks about the great commandment, who does not keep even the least.
He questions tempting, not desiring to know;
not that he might learn, but that he might deceive.
And the Lord, to the snare, answers not Fear, but Love;
for to fear belongs to slaves, but to love belongs to sons.
consider: He does not say Know God, but Love him: to know God belongs to our nature; to love him, to a religious and upright heart.
Love him with thy whole heart, that is, with thy whole understanding, leaving no place for error.
With thy whole soul, that is, with thy whole will, willing nothing contrary to him.
With thy whole mind, that is, with thy whole memory, forgetting nothing that would lessen thy sense of God.
And with thy whole strength, that all thy powers and all thy ability may serve him, and be spent upon him.
He has left no part of our life free to stand empty and turn aside to delight in some other thing.
For thy heart is bent toward Love in the measure that it is not bent toward anything else;
in whatever measure thy heart is given to some other thing, by so much is it the less for God.
Whoever sets anything beside God does him great injury, for he makes equal to God what is made.
He alone, without any rival, wills to possess our heart.
Too greedy is the man for whom God is not enough.
And no one set in mortal sin can love God, for no man can serve two masters:
the proud man loves him not, who prefers the dust of vainglory, which is nothing, to God;
nor the voluptuous man, who deserts him for a passing pleasure;
nor the greedy man, who loses him for a coin, or for nothing at all.
Remember, then, the gifts of God; all that we have, within and without, we have from him.
Too ungrateful would he be who, pondering another's kindness, did not love him.
prayer: Lord, he loves thee less who loves anything together with thee, which he loves not for thy sake.
Thou alone, without any rival, dost will to possess my heart.
Grant me to love thee with my whole heart, that it be inclined to no thing more than to thee.
With my whole soul, holding a most certain mind in thy truth, and standing firm in faith.
With my whole mind, that all my senses may be empty for thee alone.
Let whatever else comes into my soul to be loved be caught up into the one flood of love that runs wholly toward thee.
For a man is then at his best, when with his whole life he presses on toward thee, the unchangeable Good.
Glorifying thee, O Lord, as much as ever I can, thou wilt still surpass it; for thou art greater than all praise.
And so let me love my neighbor as myself, willing him, as I will for myself, to be in grace here and in glory hereafter. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Tunc est enim optimus homo, cum tota vita sua pergit in incommutabile bonum.
latinJewel.english: For a man is then at his best, when with his whole life he presses on toward the unchangeable Good.
